revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:氏長者]][[zh:氏長者]]
{{no footnotes|date=2019th year 1月|qq=}} '''Shichōja''' (氏長者) refers to the title of the head representative of a [[clan (氏)|uji (clan)]] in Japan from the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} onward. In ancient Japan, this role was known as ''Ujinokami'' (氏上). Prominent clans such as the [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto (Genji)]], [[Fujiwara clan|Fujiwara (Tōshi)]], {{ill|Tachibana clan|en|Tachibana clan|qid=Q7673718|lt=Tachibana|1=Tachibana clan}}, [[Wang clan|Ōji]], and [[Sugawara clan|Sugawara]] had their respective shichōja. The individual with the highest court Rank within the clan, as recognized by the [[:en:Imperial court|Imperial court]], was appointed as the shichōja. This position granted them authority over the [[:en:Clan deity|ujigami]], [[clan shrine|ujisha]], ancestral temples ([[ujidera]] or {{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|qid=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji|1=Bodaiji}}), and financial resources, enabling them to maintain control over clan members. == Overview == === Ancient to Nara Period === Prior to the [[Taika Reform]] (645), Japan was home to numerous clans, each governed by an ''ujinokami.'' The earliest recorded mention of ''ujinokami'' appears in the [[Nihon Shoki]], which describes an event in the 3rd year of {{ill|Emperor Tenji|en|Emperor Tenji|qid=Q314802|lt=Emperor Tenji|1=Emperor Tenji}}’s reign (664): "Swords were bestowed upon the ''ujinokami'' of large clans, and small swords upon those of smaller clans. Shields and bows were given to the ''ujinokami'' of [[Tomonomiyatsuko|Tomo no miyatsuko]] (伴造)." This decree is thought to have formalized the previously private position of ''ujinokami''. The influence of ''ujinokami'' as leaders of regional communities diminished as the [[Ritsuryō system]] incorporated them into state governance. For example, Emperor Tenmu abolished [[bemin (部曲)|bemin]] (private retainers) in 675, and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|Yōrō Code|qid=Q2575743|lt=Yōrō Code|1=Yōrō Code}} in 757 prohibited summoning clan members for private purposes. Despite these changes, during the Nara period, ''ujinokami'' retained their lifelong roles, selected by clan consensus and requiring court approval, preserving their function as clan leaders. === Heian Period and Beyond === During the Heian period, ''ujinokami'' transitioned into ''shichōja,'' with the title bestowed upon the highest-Ranking court official within the clan. This change confined the role to the aristocracy, where merely being a prominent figure within the clan was insufficient to qualify as ''shichōja.'' The position ceased to be lifelong, and Rank reversals led to frequent changes. The ''shichōja'' of the [[Fujiwara clan]] and [[Minamoto clan]] (later known as [[Fujiwara no Chōja|Tōshi Chōja]] and [[Minamoto no Chōja|Genji Chōja]]) were particularly influential. Over time, the title became largely symbolic, representing the clan's noble lineage. For example: - The Fujiwara clan linked the position to {{ill|Regent|en|Regent|qid=Q477406|lt=regents|1=Regent}} ([[:en:Sesshō|Sesshō]] and [[:en:Kanpaku|Kanpaku]]) from the [[Sekkan family]]. - The Minamoto clan’s ''shichōja'' transitioned to a monopoly held by the {{ill|Koga family|en|Koga family|qid=Q464377|lt=Koga family|1=Koga family}} and later the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. The [[Sugawara clan]] retained its ''shichōja'' title under the name "North Kyoto Chief" (北野の長者), a role filled by high-Ranking {{ill|Kugyō|en|Kugyō|qid=Q3138036|lt=Kugyō|1=Kugyō}} on a rotational basis. == Authority == The ''shichōja'' held the following responsibilities: * '''Worship and management of clan shrines and temples''' ** Examples include the [[Kasuga-taisha]] and [[Kōfuku-ji]] of the Fujiwara clan. * '''Management of [[:en:university hall|Betsusō]] (宿舎 for students)''' ** Institutions such as [[Kan’in]] (藤原氏), [[Gakkanin]] (橘氏), and [[Kōmon’in]] (和気氏) housed students from these clans. * '''Nomination of clan members for court Ranks''' ** The ''shichōja'' had the authority to recommend one member from the clan for elevation to [[Junior Fifth Rank]] or higher. This recommendation, known as ''kore sadame,'' granted the nominee privileges of nobility. == Appointment Process == Originally, ''shichōja'' were determined by their court Rank. However, after the [[:en:Hōgen Rebellion|Hōgen Rebellion]] (1156), the title required formal approval through a {{ill|proclamation|en|proclamation|qid=Q1572600|lt=proclamation|1=Proclamation}} (''senji'') by the {{ill|Emperor|en|Emperor|qid=Q39018|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor}}. Although initially viewed as a loss of autonomy, ''senji'' later became a badge of imperial endorsement. == References == {{reflist|qq=}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|Fujiwara no Chōja|ja|藤氏長者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Chōja|1=Tōshi no chōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11624448}} * {{ill|Minamoto no Chōja|ja|源氏長者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minamoto no Chōja|1=Genji no chōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11564190}} * {{ill|Kore Sadame|ja|是定|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kore Sadame|1=Kore Sadame|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11514199}} * {{ill|Sugawara no Chōja|ja|北野の長者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sugawara no Chōja|1=Kitano no chōja|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11404259}} * [[Uji (clan)]] * [[Kabane]] * [[Surname (Japan)]] * [[Clan system of Japan]]
{{translated page|ja|氏長者|version=102894450|comment=Imported full ja history}}
